Caption: Summary of the applications of MRI techniques in LIBs: Fundamental understanding and diagnosis of commercial batteries.

Schematics of several MRI concepts. (a) The Larmor precession. (b) MRI Pulse sequence of multi-slice spin-echoes. (c) Slice selection. (d) 2D Fourier-transform and k-space. The four arrows of the M x y represent the magnetization vectors located at four different positions after undergoing phase encoding with different phase-encoding gradients, exhibiting distinct phase differences. The same color represents the same phase encoding step.

(a) Schematic of the electrochemical cell. (b) 1D 7Li NMR images of the cell in (a), to which a constant current of 30 μA was applied. Two lines are highlighted: one before the current was applied ( t = 0; black curve) and the other after the application of the current at t = 17.8 h (blue curve). (c) Concentration profile at t = 0 normalized to the RF sensitivity profile used to account for the inhomogeneity of the RF field (Reprinted from Ref. [31]. The figure was reproduced with permission from American Chemical Society). (d) Schematic representation of the pseudo-3D experiment design and output 3D spectrum. (e) Steady-state lithium salt concentration (blue triangles) and diffusivity (maroon circles) profiles in 1 M LiPF6/EC: DMC (1:1 v/v) at a current density of 9 A m−2 (Reprinted from Ref. [32]. The figure was reproduced with permission from American Chemical Society).

2D cross sections of the pristine and the cycled Li10GeP2S12 electrolytes in a symmetric Li/Li10GeP2S12/Li cell. (a–c) Cross sections taken from the top, middle, and bottom, respectively, of the acquired 3D 7Li MRI image of the pristineLi10GeP2S12 pellet. (d–f) Corresponding cross sections of the Li10GeP2S12 pellet after 3 days of electrochemical cycling. The color bar indicating the relative Li signal intensity is shown on the bottom right (Reprinted from Ref. [44]. The figure was reproduced with permission from American Chemical Society).

(a–c) In situ 1H 3D FLASH imaging results of a Li|Li symmetric cell. (a) 2D slices from four time points. (b) Segmented images of the results from a, where I threshold = 0.2I max . (c) 3D segmented images of the same time points with an additional Gaussian filter applied to smoothen the visualization (Reprinted from Ref. [49]. The figure was reproduced with permission from Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America). (d, f) 7Li three-dimensional MRI of Li|Li bag cell in its pristine state (d) and after charging (f). (e, g) 7Li chemical-shift images of the Li-metal bag cell in the pristine state (e) and after charging (g) (Reprinted from Ref. [50]. The figure was reproduced with permission from Springer Nature).

(a) MRI time series showing the evolution of the 7Li electrolyte concentration profile (top) and the 7Li chemical shift image of the metal (bottom) for the cell (Reprinted from Ref. [51]. The figure was reproduced with permission from American Chemical Society). (b) Schematic of a symmetrical Li−LLZTO−Li cell depicting the orientation with respect to the external magnetic field, B 0 , during the 7Li CSI experiment (Reproduced from Ref. [52] under the Creative Commons License).

(a–c) 7Li Scanning ISIS of the LCO/LTO battery. (a) Series of spectra obtained for horizontal slices of thickness 100 mm in the battery. (b, c) Comparison of the NMR spectroscopic images for the LCO/LTO battery obtained with (b) the S–ISIS method and (c) the standard CSI (Reproduced from Ref. [66] under the Creative Commons License). (d) Schematics of the in situ Graphite-Li cell; (e) axial 7Li MR images which reveal the lithium concentration profile inside the graphite electrode (Reprinted from Ref. [67]. The figure was reproduced with permission from American Chemical Society).

Magnetic field map measurements for the fully charged cells. (a) Sample placement, and image orientation; (b) field maps measured for the cells. Field maps are referenced here to the empty holder, giving an absolute field map for the Li-ion cell (Reproduced from Ref. [70] under the Creative Commons License). (c) y-z slices through 3D io-SPRITE maps of two damaged iPhone-5 batteries (Reprinted from Ref. [71]. The figure was reproduced with permission from Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America). (d, e) ΔB DC maps of a Nokia cell at SoCs of 88% (‘healthy’) and 178% (overcharged), respectively. Locations of three most intense ΔB DC deviations are indicated with dashed circles in (e) and (f) (Reprinted from Ref. [72]. The figure was reproduced with permission from Royal Society of Chemistry).
